design_philosophy.core_loop: Answer survey questions correctly to earn money and accumulate points; compete against computer opponent or second player to reach winning threshold ($200 or 300 points depending on version)
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Speed vs. accuracy under time pressure; knowledge of common survey answers vs. attempting unconventional but valid responses
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Participate in the Family Feud game show experience as a contestant; test cultural knowledge against 100 surveyed opinions
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Game is strictly faithful recreation of TV show format rather than independent game design; validates answers through show logic (accepts reasonable similes and misspellings), prioritizing authenticity to source material over rigid pattern matching

difficulty.continues_system: Game allows player to choose to continue or stop after winning; champion automatically retires after accumulating $20,000 or 5 consecutive wins
difficulty.checkpointing: Save codes provided after each win in later GameTek versions (SNES/Genesis/3DO) to resume progress
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High agency: player controls pacing (can quit anytime), can choose which answers to attempt, controls risk tolerance via continue decision

progression.unlock_mechanism: Progression through sequential game rounds (2 Single Rounds, 1 Double Round, 1 Triple Round in original; later versions add Bullseye Round); Fast Money unlocked upon winning main game
replayability_hooks: Multiple difficulty levels via opponent AI
Fast Money round with different question pools
Champion retirement system creates narrative progression (stopping point at $20,000 or 5 wins)

world_building_techniques: Host character (Richard Dawson lookalike in NES version) provides personality and TV show authenticity
Realistic game show set design recreates Richard Dawson era (1976-1985) on NES
Flavor text excuses when computer wins Fast Money ('tax considerations', 'birth in the family') add comedic flavor
Survey data implicitly builds world through 100 people's opinions on everyday topics

environmental_storytelling: Game show set design signals entertainment/family game context
Survey questions reveal cultural attitudes and common knowledge expectations

sources_used: wikipedia: Family Feud (video game series) - NES section, GameTek versions gameplay section
